original_hypothesis.title: Permissionless staking implementation initialization allows attacker-controlled staking instances with arbitrary parameters
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: StakingToken.initialize() and StakingNativeToken.initialize() are external and have no access control beyond StakingBase._initialize single-init check (serviceRegistry != address(0)). Any actor can deploy these implementations standalone (or any proxy pointing to them) and initialize with arbitrary parameters (serviceRegistry, activityChecker, rewardsPerSecond, proxyHash, etc.). This can create malicious staking targets that appear valid to downstream systems depending on verification configuration, potentially siphoning emissions (if voted for) or griefing distribution flows.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The system explicitly allows permissionless staking contract creation via factory, but standalone deployments plus weak/optional verifier settings can expand the attack surface. Realistic if verifier is unset or misconfigured, or if consumers do not strictly require factory verification on all chains.
deployment_assumptions: StakingVerifier / StakingFactory verification is optional or not enforced on a target chain (verifier == 0 or implementationsCheck == false)
VoteWeighting allows nominee addition for arbitrary staking targets (as designed)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: StakingFactory constructor allows verifier to be zero; StakingVerifier implementationsCheck can be false; no deployment evidence provided.

proof_of_signal.lines: initialize(...) is external; calls _initialize(...) then sets stakingToken/serviceRegistryTokenUtility with no caller restriction
proof_of_signal.pattern: external initializer without ownership/role guard (only single-init)

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a staking instance (standalone or via any proxy) pointing to these implementations
Downstream emissions routing (L2 dispensers / L1 processor) accepts targets based on weak verification settings or social layer listing

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker can craft staking contract behavior/params to maximize emissions or create denial-of-service via invalid configs

analysis_questions: Is the verifier always enabled (non-zero) in production StakingFactory and on L2 dispenser verification paths?
Can a non-factory staking instance still pass verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount (e.g., verifier==0 path)?
Are nominees in VoteWeighting curated off-chain or purely permissionless on-chain?
validation_requirements: Simulate a malicious staking contract instance and check acceptance by StakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount() across verifier configurations
End-to-end test: VoteWeighting nominee -> Dispenser claim -> L2 deposit -> staking target receives OLAS

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a staking contract instance initialized to appear compatible, adds it as a nominee in VoteWeighting, attracts votes or exploits governance assumptions, then receives emissions via L2 dispenser deposits if verification is weak.
prerequisites: Verifier not strictly enforced OR misconfigured
Sufficient votes directed to attacker nominee

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Diversion of staking emissions to attacker-controlled staking target; potential long-lived drain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: veOLAS voters and emission recipients; protocol inflation allocation correctness
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires nominee removal and/or verifier tightening; may take time due to vote/remove constraints

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Need tests asserting non-factory instances cannot receive emissions under intended production verifier settings.

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: veOLAS voting power (or coordination) to route emissions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor new nominees and whether targets are factory-verified

validation_result.impact_summary: Non-factory staking instances cannot receive emissions because StakingFactory.verifyInstance() checks mapInstanceParams, which is only populated during createStakingInstance(). Standalone deployments always fail this check regardless of verifier configuration.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that the lack of access control on StakingToken.initialize() and StakingNativeToken.initialize() (beyond the single-init guard) allows attackers to create malicious staking instances that receive emissions. While the proof_of_signal is correct that these functions are external without caller restrictions, the downstream emission verification in StakingFactory prevents non-factory instances from receiving any funds.

The critical safeguard is in StakingFactory.verifyInstance() at lines checking mapInstanceParams[instance].implementation. This mapping is ONLY populated inside createStakingInstance(), which atomically deploys a StakingProxy via CREATE2 and then initializes it. Any standalone deployment — whether the implementation itself or a custom proxy — will have mapInstanceParams[instance].implementation == address(0), causing verifyInstance() to return false.

This means verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount() returns 0 for any non-factory instance, regardless of whether verifier is set to address(0) or implementationsCheck is false. On L2, DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData() withholds the full amount when limitAmount == 0. On L1, EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit() reverts with TargetEmissionsZero.

The initialize() functions being publicly callable is intentional for the proxy pattern — the factory atomically deploys and initializes in a single transaction, preventing front-running. The implementation contract itself being initializable by anyone is a cosmetic issue (common in UUPS patterns) but has no security impact since the implementation address is never registered in mapInstanceParams.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: StakingFactory.verifyInstance() (StakingFactory.sol) provides the critical gate: it checks mapInstanceParams[instance].implementation != address(0) before any verifier-dependent checks. This mapping is exclusively populated in createStakingInstance(). DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData() uses a low-level call to verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount and withholds funds when limitAmount == 0. EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit() hard-reverts when limitAmount == 0.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function REFUTES the hypothesis: the check 'if (implementation == address(0)) return false' ensures that any instance not deployed through createStakingInstance() (which is the only place mapInstanceParams is populated) will always fail verification, regardless of verifier configuration.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker deploys standalone StakingToken/StakingNativeToken or custom proxy
2. Attacker calls initialize() with arbitrary parameters (succeeds - no caller check)
3. Attacker adds the instance as nominee via VoteWeighting.addNomineeEVM() (succeeds - permissionless)
4. Votes are directed to the nominee, claimStakingIncentives is called
5. Dispenser calculates staking incentive and sends to deposit processor
10. _processData withholds the full amount (limitAmount == 0), no deposit to target
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path is blocked at step 8. StakingFactory.verifyInstance() checks mapInstanceParams[instance].implementation, which is only set during createStakingInstance(). For any standalone deployment, this value is address(0), causing verification to fail. The funds are withheld on L2 (or the tx reverts on L1), preventing the attacker from receiving any emissions.

deployment_assumptions_checked: StakingVerifier / StakingFactory verification is optional or not enforced on a target chain (verifier == 0 or implementationsCheck == false)
VoteWeighting allows nominee addition for arbitrary staking targets (as designed)

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires a non-factory staking instance to pass StakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(). This is impossible because verifyInstance() first checks mapInstanceParams[instance].implementation != address(0), and mapInstanceParams is only populated by createStakingInstance(). Even with verifier == address(0), a standalone deployment cannot pass this check. The scenario where an attacker uses the factory itself (permissionless createStakingInstance with verifier == 0) is a different attack vector not covered by this hypothesis, and would be a governance/configuration concern rather than a code defect in initialize().
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The system correctly prevents non-factory staking instances from receiving emissions through the mapInstanceParams check in StakingFactory.verifyInstance(). The lack of access control on initialize() is a design choice for the proxy pattern, not a vulnerability.
recommended_mitigations: Consider adding a constructor that calls _initialize with dummy values on the implementation contract to prevent cosmetic initialization of the implementation (standard UUPS practice, defense-in-depth only)
Document that the factory registration (mapInstanceParams) is the primary access control, not the initialize() function
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy StakingToken implementation. 2. Call initialize() directly on it with arbitrary params. 3. Add it as nominee via VoteWeighting.addNomineeEVM(). 4. Direct votes to it. 5. Call claimStakingIncentives. 6. Verify that on L2, _processData withholds the full amount because verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount returns 0 for the non-factory instance.
uncertainties: The permissionless nature of StakingFactory.createStakingInstance() with verifier == 0 is a separate concern not addressed by this hypothesis but could allow creation of staking instances with arbitrary parameters through the factory itself
additional_testing_needed: Test that standalone deployments (not via factory) always get limitAmount == 0 from verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount
Test the permissionless factory path with verifier == 0 to assess if factory-created instances with malicious parameters can receive emissions
